audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a student talking to a housing officer about living with a homestay family.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Yes, what can I do for you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: My friend is in homestay, and she really enjoys it.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: So I'd like to join a family as well.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, so let me get some details.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: My name is Keiko Yuichini.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Could you spell your family name for me?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: It's Yuichini.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That's YUICHINI.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The student's surname is Yuichini.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Yuichini has been written on the form.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Yes, what can I do for you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: My friend is in homestay, and she really enjoys it.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: So I'd like to join a family as well.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, so let me get some details.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: My name is Keiko Yuichini.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Could you spell your family name for me?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: It's Yuichini.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That's YUICHINI.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And your first name?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: It's Keiko.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: KEIKO.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That's Keiko Yuichini... OK...
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: And you're female, and your nationality?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I'm Japanese.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: And could I see your passport please?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Here it is.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, your passport number is JO6337, and you're how old?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I'm 28 years old.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Now you live at one of the colleges, which one?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Willow College, um, room 21C.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Right, 21C Willow College. And how long are you planning on staying with homestay?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: About four months, longer if I like it.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And what course are you enrolled in?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Well, I've enrolled for 20 weeks in the advanced English studies, because I need help with my writing, and I'm nearly at the end of my first 5 week course.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Do you have any preference for a family with children or without children?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I prefer, I mean, I like young children, but I'd like to be with older people.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: You know, adults, someone around my age.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: OK, and what about pets?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I am a veterinarian, so that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: The more the better.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: All right, now what about you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Are you a vegetarian, or do you have any special food requirements?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: No, I am not a vegetarian, but I don't eat a lot of meat.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I really like seafood.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: And what are your hobbies?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I like reading and going to the movies.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Do you play any sports?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Yes, I joined the handball team.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: But I didn't like that, so I stopped playing.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Now I play tennis on the weekend with my friends.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: All right, let's see.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Name, age, uh, now the location.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Are you familiar with the public transport system?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: No, I'm not really, because I have been living on campus.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I've been to the city a few times on the bus, but they are always late.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: What about the trains?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I like catching the train.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: They are much faster.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Now, let me go check on the computer and see who I've got.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Um. Listen, leave it with me.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: I'll check my records, and I'll give you details this afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Thank you for helping me.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: It's a pleasure.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICER

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

What mode of transport does the student prefer?

test_text.title: A Tour of the Bicentennial Park

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a guide giving a tour of a park.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Welcome to all of you.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Can everybody see and hear me?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'm Sally, your guide for this tour of the Bicentennial Park.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I hope that you're all wearing your most comfortable shoes, and that you can keep up the pace.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So, let's get underway on our tour around this wonderful park.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'll start today with some general background information.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There used to be a lot of factories in this area until the 1960s.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Creating the park required the demolition of lots of derelict buildings on the site.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So most of the exciting park space all around you was originally warehouses and storehouses.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The idea of building a public park here was first discussed when a property developer proposed a high-rise housing development, but the local community wasn't happy.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: If the land was to be cleaned up, they wanted to use the site for recreation.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Residents wanted open space for outdoor activities, rather than housing or even an indoor sports complex.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Now to the Bicentennial Park itself, it has two areas, a nature reserve, and a formal park with manmade features and gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The tall blue-and-white building in front of us is called The Tower, and is the center point for the formal gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: It stands 12 meters high.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: So follow me up the stairs to where we can take advantage of the fantastic views.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the tour, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, here we are at the top of The Tower, and we're gonna look at the view from each direction.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Out to the east, the large buildings about a kilometer away are on the Olympic site.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There's an indoor arena for gymnastics, a stadium for track and field, and a swimming pool for races and synchronized swimming, and also diving.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: If you look carefully down there, you can see the train lines.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The Olympic site has its own station to encourage the use of public transport.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There is also a car park, but it only holds a limited number of cars.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The formal park has some specially created water features.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: If you look out here to the south, you can see a circular ornamental pond, and around to the west, you can relax and sit on a bench to smell the flowers in the rose garden.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And finally up to the north, if you look in front of you now.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There's a lake with a small island in the center.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: You can hire rowing boats at the boat shed, which you can't see from here, but if you look through the trees, you can see the cafe, which has lovely views across the water.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK, let's climb down now.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: We will go now and have a look at the nature reserve section of the park, which has opened up natural wetland to the public.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The mangroves have been made more accessible to visitors by the boardwalk built during the park's upgrade.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: You'd think that people would come here to look at the unusual plant life of the area, but in fact it's more often used for cycling, and is very popular with the local clubs.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: This is the far end of the park, and over there you can see the Frog Pond, a natural feature here long before the park was designed.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Just next to it, we have our outdoor classroom.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: A favorite spot for school parties, the area is now most often used by primary schools for biology lessons.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And finally, let's pass by the waterbird refuge.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: This area is in a sheltered part of the estuary.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That's why the park's viewing shelter is a favorite spot for bird watchers who can use it to spy through binoculars.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: You can watch a variety of waterbirds, but most visitors expect to see black swans when they come to the shelter.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: You might spot one yourself right now.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Well, here we are back at our starting point, the Visitor Center.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Well, here we are back at our starting point, the Visitor Center.
utterances.speaker: SALLY

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Preparing for the Presentation on the Islands' Education System

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students discussing a geography presentation they're going to give.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Remind me, Trevor, how long is the presentation?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Dr White said three per hour.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: So about 20 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, it'll be 15 minutes per presentation.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: And five minutes for questions?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: And is this one gonna be assessed?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: No, not this time round, because it's the first one, you know.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, good news.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Well, Trevor, what are we going to include?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, do you think we ought to give some historical background?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Oh, no, definitely not.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: We won't have time.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: OK, but I think we ought to say something about the geographical location.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: 'Cause not a lot of people know where the islands are.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Yes, OK.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: I'll take notes, shall I?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, that'll be a help.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: So geographical location, then we ought to give an overview of the whole education system.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Shouldn't we say something about the economy?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: You know agricultural produce, minerals, and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, Dr White said we shouldn't go into that sort of detail.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: But it's pretty important when you think about it.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: You know, because it does influence the education system.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Look, let's think about that one later, shall we?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Let's see how we're doing for time.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: OK, so general overview of education.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: And then the role of English language.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. No, that goes in the language policy seminar, don't you remember?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Are you sure?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Positive.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: All right, so those are the topics we're going to be covering.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We need to think about what to prepare.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Dr White said he wanted us to use plenty of visuals and things, and we might as well try them out when we're not being assessed.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, the most important thing is the overhead projector.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: We'll get that from the media room.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Must remember to book it.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well we'll need a map of course.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. Probably two, one of the islands large scale, and one of West Africa.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well the West African one is no problem.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: There's one in the resources room.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah, of course, the resources room.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: The islands are gonna be more of a problem.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Tell you what, there's a very clear map of Santiago in that tourist brochure I showed you last week.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Don't you remember it?
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: We can just use the tourist brochure.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: We also need statistics on several different things.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Literacy rates, yes, and school places.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: How about the encyclopedia?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Nah, not up to date enough.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Uh. Why don't we call the embassy?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Oh, someone's enthusiastic.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Well, if something's worth doing...
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: I know it's worth doing well, OK.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: We can find out statistics on school places from them as well.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. Might as well.
audioscript.speaker: JULIE

audioscript.text: Look, Julie, it's almost time for our tutorials.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: We can meet again on Monday.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: But we need to prepare some stuff before then...
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Monosodium Glutamate: A Flavour Enhancer Explored

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear part of a lecture about a chemical substance called monosodium glutamate.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In today's lecture, I'm going to talk about monosodium glutamate, or MSG as it's more commonly known.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, MSG as you probably know is a flavor enhancer,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: which is used particularly in Chinese and Japanese cooking.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, I am going to explore why it is so popular in these cuisines.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And more importantly, how does it enhance the flavor of food?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main reason why MSG is more commonly used in Japanese meals is tradition.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For many thousands of years, the Japanese have incorporated a type of seaweed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Known as kombu in their cooking, as they discovered it had the ability to make food taste better.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it wasn't until 1908 that the ingredient in kombu which was responsible for the improvement in flavor, was actually discovered to be glutamate by scientists working there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From 1908 until 1956 glutamate was produced commercially in Japan by a very slow and expensive means of extraction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was in 1956 that the speed of the process was improved, and industrial production increased dramatically, and still continues to increase to this day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, hundreds of thousands of tonnes of MSG are produced all over the world today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what exactly is MSG?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well monosodium glutamate contains 78.2% glutamate, 12.2% sodium and 9.6% water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Glutamate is an amino acid that can be found naturally in all protein containing foods.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So this includes food such as meat and cheese.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It is widely known that Chinese and Japanese food contains MSG.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But many people don't seem to be aware that it is also used in foods in other parts of the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, it is found in commercially made Italian pizzas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In American fast food, and in Britain, MSG is used in things like potato crisps.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, how exactly does MSG work?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, in the western world, we commonly talk of four tastes, and I'm sure you're all familiar with the concepts of sweet, sour, bitter, and salt.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, in 1908, Kikunae Ikeda identified a fifth taste, and it is thought that MSG intensifies this naturally occurring 'taste' in some food.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It does make perfect evolutionary sense that we should have the ability to detect or taste glutamate, because it is the amino acid which is most common in natural foods.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: John Prescott, an associate professor at the University of Chicago, suggests that this fifth taste serves a purpose just as the other tastes do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He suggests that it signals to us the presence of protein in food, in the same way that sweetness indicates that a food contains energy giving carbohydrates.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Bitterness, he says, alerts us to toxins in the food, while sourness warns us of spoilage, and saltiness signals the presence of minerals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what else do we know about this fifth taste?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The speaker says the main topic of the lecture is
What change occurred in the manufacture of glutamate in 1956?
options: It began to be manufactured on a large scale.
The Japanese began extracting it from natural sources.
It became much more expensive to produce.

body.title: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
